statement.markdown: The goal of this problem is to debug a faulty implementation of the Euclidean algorithm, which is used to calculate the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers. Given a function `solve` that takes two integers `a` and `b`, your task is to fix the bug in this function to make it correctly compute the GCD of `a` and `b`. The function should return the correct GCD value.
statement.outputFormat: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of a and b
statement.notes: Ensure that your function handles both positive and negative integers.

hints.text: The Euclidean algorithm uses repeated division and remainder calculations to compute the GCD. Identify the incorrect logic in the buggy implementation.

editorial.title: Fixing the Euclidean Algorithm
editorial.intuition: The Euclidean algorithm works by repeatedly replacing `a` with `b` and `b` with the remainder of `a` divided by `b` until `b` becomes 0. At that point, `a` will be the GCD of the original `a` and `b`.
editorial.bruteForce: One way to solve this problem is to implement the Euclidean algorithm correctly. However, a more efficient approach is to identify the incorrect logic in the buggy implementation and fix it.
editorial.optimalSolution: To fix the bug, replace the line `if (a < b) return b;` with `if (a < b) { a = b; b = a % b; }` to ensure the correct implementation of the Euclidean algorithm.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This corrected implementation correctly computes the GCD of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run to verify the correctness of the solution

functionSignature.cpp: Method signature string, e.g. 'int solve(int a, int b);'
functionSignature.java: Method signature string, e.g. 'int solve(int a, int b);'
functionSignature.python: Method signature string, e.g. 'def solve(self, a: int, b: int) -> int:'
